cells.data: Using Mocha and Chai
npm install mocha chai --save-dev
First, write a canary test to make sure all is installed correctly
The name of the test should clearly and concisely express the intention of the test and convey what’s expected of the code being tested.
Each test will require an instance of the function that is being tested. To remove the need of having to continue to creating the instance, it can be added in the beforeEach

In general, tests follow an Arrange-Act-Assert pattern. Best practice has it where each is separate with a line in between.
arrange - sets up what code is needed for the code under the test
act - exercises the code under test
assert - verifies that the code fulfills the expectations.
NOTE: If the test runs a multitude of lines, this is telling you that the test is too complicated and needs to be cut down
Start with a failing test
The next step is to write the minimal amount of code to get it to pass
To better test this, we should be able to pass different values in to get it to pass, stretching the function to its limits
Changing the code now to expand the ability to make this pass is adding the ability for the function to handle these changing conditions

Keep in mind when writing tests
  • both tests and test suites should have a single responsibilty. If a group of functions work closely together, keep them in one test suite. If not, separate them into separate suites.
  • Test should be relevant and verify the correct behavior of the code. The tests we write describe, express, and document the code behavior.
When writing tests, the first few steps will help to shape the interface of the function - that is the skin. The tests that follow will help shape the implentation of the guts of the function.
Avoid the common antipattern of writing tests for getting and setting state. Instead, start by testing interesting and useful behavior. Let any necessary state fall in place along the way

A good starting point is to develop code through a series of positive, negative, and exception tests.
  • positive tests exercise and verify that the code does what is expected when its preconditions are met
  • negative tests verify that the code gracefully behaves when things don’t go as expected - that is, when its preconditions or input are not valid
  • s verify that if code is supposed to throw an exception, it does in fact.
